blocks.content: Danny Pruett and his crew left. You can't stay somewhere you're losing the room.
The four of them stood at the base and looked up.
The iron was rough under his hands, warm from the sun, rust coming off in faint reddish-brown stripes. He tested each rung before trusting it — about as careful as Kit ever got.

Eli was right behind him, then Savannah — talking herself into it out loud, a low murmur that was half pep talk and half threat directed at the ladder. Daisy came last, moving steady, not looking down.

The wind showed up the moment they got above the tree line. Down in town the summer air had no interest in moving. Up here it was alive — field grass and something cool and distant, the suggestion of rain.
Kit pulled himself up onto the catwalk and stood.

Millhaven spread out in every direction. The new water tower on the west side. First Baptist's white spire. The Tasty Freeze sign lit up even in daylight. Callaway Park's diamond. The courthouse copper dome. The long slow line of trees where the creek ran. And past it all, open country going on as far as the summer haze would let you see.
He stood there a moment before any of them reached him.
